text:
Software Engineering is the systematic application of engineering principles to the design, development, testing, deployment, and maintenance of software systems.

SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle) is the process used by software teams to plan, create, test, and deliver software. Common SDLC models include Waterfall, Agile, Scrum, Kanban, and Spiral.

Agile Methodology is an iterative approach to software development. Work is broken into small cycles called sprints (usually 2 weeks). Key Agile values: individuals over processes, working software over documentation, customer collaboration over contract negotiation, responding to change over following a plan.

Scrum is a framework under Agile. Key roles in Scrum: Product Owner (defines requirements), Scrum Master (removes blockers), Development Team (builds the product). Key ceremonies: Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective.

Waterfall Model is a linear sequential SDLC model. Phases: Requirements → System Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance. Best for projects with well-defined, fixed requirements.

Version Control is the practice of tracking and managing changes to code. Git is the most widely used version control system. Key Git concepts: repository, branch, commit, merge, pull request, conflict resolution.

GitHub and GitLab are platforms for hosting Git repositories and enabling team collaboration through pull requests, code reviews, and CI/CD pipelines.

CI/CD stands for Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment. CI means developers frequently merge code into a shared repository with automated testing. CD means code is automatically deployed to production after passing tests. Popular CI/CD tools: GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI, CircleCI.

Software Testing types: Unit Testing (testing individual functions), Integration Testing (testing combined modules), System Testing (testing the full system), User Acceptance Testing (UAT — end user validates the system), Regression Testing (ensuring new changes don't break existing features).

Design Patterns are reusable solutions to common software design problems. Categories: Creational (e.g., Singleton, Factory), Structural (e.g., Adapter, Decorator), Behavioral (e.g., Observer, Strategy).

SOLID Principles are five object-oriented design principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion. These help build maintainable, scalable code.

Database types: Relational (SQL) databases store data in tables with relationships — examples: MySQL, PostgreSQL. Non-relational (NoSQL) databases store data as documents, key-value pairs, or graphs — examples: MongoDB, Redis, Firebase.

API (Application Programming Interface) allows software systems to communicate. REST API uses HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and returns JSON. GraphQL is an alternative that lets clients request exactly the data they need.

Software Architecture refers to the high-level structure of a software system. Common architectures: Monolithic (single deployable unit), Microservices (independent small services), Serverless (functions triggered by events), MVC (Model-View-Controller pattern).

Code Review is the practice of having peers examine code before merging. Benefits: catches bugs early, enforces standards, knowledge sharing across the team.

Technical Debt refers to the implied cost of future rework caused by choosing quick solutions now instead of better approaches. It accumulates when code quality is sacrificed for speed.

Documentation in software engineering includes inline code comments, README files, API documentation, architecture diagrams, and user manuals. Good documentation improves maintainability and onboarding.
